Hi all! I’m a Computational Media PhD student at the Expressive Intelligence Studio at UC Santa Cruz, working on approachable and playful mixed-initiative (AI+human creative collaboration) tools to augment human creativity. Like a lot of you, Bret Victor’s work was probably the main thing that got me into future-of-programming stuff, but I’m also inspired by games like Spore that place the process of creating stuff at the center of the play experience. More recently I’ve been thinking a lot about the problem of how to empower people to shape their digital social environments to suit their personal and community-specific needs.

I’m nominally an “AI researcher”, but I think the approaches to AI that are currently in vogue make a critical mistake: they focus way too much on _guessing_ what people want rather than _asking_ them what they want (and giving them the languages they need to answer properly). As a result, all of the AI problems I’m interested in end up also being HCI and/or PL problems to some extent, and my work keeps overlapping with future-of-programming stuff in interesting ways.

Some of you probably recognize me from Twitter; I’m <https://twitter.com/maxkreminski>. My personal website and blog is at <https://mkremins.github.io>.

attachments.title: Max Kreminski (@maxkreminski) | Twitter
attachments.text: The latest Tweets from Max Kreminski (@maxkreminski). Using computers to enable & augment human creativity. Also expressive AI, procgen, games & play. Computational Media PhD student @UCSC. (they/them). Santa Cruz, CA

I ran a small snippet of code, and then used the spotter to look for documentation about that code.

Based on issues with Slack brought up in this thread, the first thing that comes to mind is Discourse <https://www.discourse.org> (which was mentioned earlier but not really discussed). E.g., less emphasis on notifications and easier to highlight evergreen content. That's really more a difference of a forum versus a chat though (Discourse is forum software). I wonder if a having second place (that's a forum or maybe even a wiki?) would be better rather than switching to another chat platform? Personally I find all chat software to have all the issues people brought up here.

attachments.title: Discourse - Civilized Discussion
attachments.text: Discourse is modern forum software for your community. Use it as a mailing list, discussion forum, long-form chat room, and more!

Cool! Hope this is all gentle enough:
• "I'm do the important" → "I'm doing the important"?
• You mention "the podcast" under "Community" as though it's something you've mentioned already, but it's the only time you use the word "podcast" on the whole page. I would recommend highlighting the podcast and describing it explicitly, since it is (from my perspective) one of the most approachable and substantial parts of your work.
• The links in the intro paragraph are to references / inspirations. I expected them, from context, to be links to your work (especially the one that said "I build microworlds and objects-to-think-with", since it's supposed to be about /you/). Perhaps these could instead be links to your work, and you could link to the inspirations in a section that was more about them?

(I'll post below if I come up with more bullet points.)
